complex_text: Here, we adopt the noncartographic approach. Following Slioussar , we assume that the preverbal element is often construed as the topic of the sentence, but that this does not necessarily correspond to a single syntactic position. We also assume that there is no dedicated position for contrastive focus, given that contrastively focused elements can occur in different positions in the sentence. We propose that a fronted object is always fronted for reasons of topichood: it is what the sentence is about. A preverbal subject can also be a topic, but need not be: since SVO is the default word order, an SVO sentence can be uttered completely out of the blue, with all elements in the sentence being new information, and equally .

Our conclusion is that in Russian, as in English, it is possible to derive inverse scope via covert QR, independently of word order and prosody considerations. Just as in English, covert QR is dispreferred in Russian for reasons of processing.

simplified_text:
The Scope of Quantifier Phrases in Russian

Quantifier phrases in Russian are important parts of language. They help us talk about numbers and amounts. This study looked at how these phrases work in Russian.

In Russian, quantifier phrases can be used in different ways. They can be used to talk about big groups or small groups. They can also be used to talk about exact numbers or approximate numbers. This study found that Russian speakers use these phrases in different ways depending on the situation.

The study also looked at how Russian speakers use these phrases in different parts of speech. For example, they can be used as adjectives or adverbs. This study found that the way Russian speakers use these phrases can affect the meaning of the sentence.

Overall, this study showed that quantifier phrases in Russian are important and can be used in different ways.

complex_text: We hypothesize that contrastive focus leads to reconstruction and can thus potentially result in the inverse scope interpretation of the sentence. We follow Ionin’s proposal that topics do not reconstruct: since the topic is what the sentence is about, it should be interpreted first. On the other hand, when the preverbal element is contrastively focused, it is not the topic; following Slioussar , we assume that a contrastively focused element is fronted purely for stylistic reasons, rather than for IS reasons, and hence is subject to reconstruction.4

complex_text: An experimental investigation of quantifier scope in Russian SVO and OVS sentences, in which the factors of word order, prosody, information structure, and indefinite form are manipulated, shows that native Russian speakers have a preference for surface scope under neutral prosody, though this preference is more pronounced with odin ‘one’ indefinites than with dva ‘two’ indefinites. Furthermore, contrastive focus on the fronted object QP in OVS order is found to facilitate the inverse scope reading, but contrastive focus on the subject in SVO order is not. These findings have implications for the syntactic analysis of noncanonical word order in Russian and support the link between contrastive focus and scope reconstruction in Russian .

But this is more true when the sentence starts with "one" instead of "two". We also found that when we put the object at the beginning of the sentence, it helps us understand the sentence in a different way. But when we put the subject at the beginning, it doesn't help. These results are important for understanding how Russian sentences are structured and how we use emphasis to understand the sentence.

complex_text: This derivation successfully accounts for the fact that OVS structures obviate a Weak Crossover violation, yet do not allow anaphor binding by the scrambled object . However, as far as we can see, it runs into a problem with regard to scope interpretation. The derivation in (10) cannot explain why the default scope reading for OVS sentences is object  subject: in (10b), the scrambled object does not c-command the subject, so it cannot scope over the subject. Assuming that the moved VP in (10b) leaves behind a trace of type e, t, the VPmovement is semantically vacuous, and the subject still scopes over the object at LF. We do not see any straightforward way of deriving the object  subject scope for the configuration in (10b). Given that the object  subject reading is the default reading for OVS order (both according to existing accounts of Russian scope and according to the data reported here), we do not consider this account further.

In (10b), the scrambled object doesn't have control over the subject, so it can't be the main idea. The moved sentence part in (10b) leaves behind a reminder of the type of word it is, but this doesn't change the meaning, and the subject is still the main idea. We can't think of a simple way to make the object the main idea in this sentence structure. Since the object is the main idea in OVS sentences (both in existing Russian sentence studies and in the data we collected), we won't consider this idea further.

complex_text: 2.3.1 Information Structure and Syntactic Position An important question debated in the literature on Russian word order is whether discourse functions such as topic and focus correspond to particular syntactic positions . “Cartographic” approaches , which include those of King and Dyakonova , posit specific functional categories in the C-domain for Topic and Focus. In contrast, noncartographic approaches, which include those of Bailyn , Junghanns and Zybatow , Sekerina , Pereltsvaig , and Slioussar , among many others, do not posit dedicated Topic or Focus positions. As Bailyn discusses, focused elements can potentially occur anywhere in the sentence, both preverbally and postverbally, while topics can potentially be in the CP- or the vP-domain.

Some researchers think that the way we structure our sentences is connected to specific places in the sentence. For example, if we want to talk about something important, we might put it at the beginning of the sentence. Others disagree and say that we can put important information anywhere in the sentence.

For example, some researchers say that we can put important information before or after the main verb in the sentence. They also think that we can put the main topic of the sentence in different parts of the sentence, like at the beginning or in the middle.

complex_text: languages, ed. by Cornelia Ebert and Stefan Hinterwimmer, 75–103. Dordrecht: Springer. Ionin, Tania, Cornelia Ebert, and Britta Stolterfoht. 2011. One indefinite scopes out of islands: An experi
simplified_text:
Languages are a way for people to communicate. 

Sometimes, we use words to talk about things that are not right in front of us. This is called "scope". 

Imagine you're talking about a friend who is at the park. You're not at the park, but you're still talking about it. That's scope! 

Now, sometimes we use special words to help us talk about things that are far away. These words are called "islands". 

For example, if you're talking about a friend who is at the park, the park is like an "island" that's separate from where you are. 

So, when we use scope and islands together, it's like using a special map to help us talk about things that are far away.

complex_text: Phonological Resolution of Syntactic Feature Conflict Author, pp. 751-773 Published by: Linguistic Society of America Stable URL: http://www.jstor.org/stable/415171 Accessed: 17/05/2009 16:21
simplified_text:
When Words Sound the Same but Mean Different Things

Sometimes, words in a sentence can sound the same but have different meanings. This is called a "syntactic feature conflict." It's like trying to decide which way to go when two roads have the same name.

Our brains have to figure out which word means what in this situation. This is called "phonological resolution." It's like solving a puzzle to understand what's being said.

For example, if someone says "I'm going to the store," we need to decide which "store" they mean. Is it the grocery store, the toy store, or the bookstore? We use clues like the context and the person's tone to figure it out.

Phonological resolution is important because it helps us understand each other better. It's like having a special tool to decode the meaning of words.

As with (8), it is possible to derive inverse scope either via reconstruction of the object (9b) or via covert QR of the subject (9c). We note that the derivation in (9) makes exactly the same predictions for scope interpretation as the one in (8), so data on (im)possible or (dis)preferred scope readings would not be able to tease these two accounts apart. There's another way to look at this. The object can be in a special spot called Spec,TP, but the subject can still move to the end of the sentence. Finally a brief discussion of non-contrastive topics is provided.

complex_text: The question that naturally arises is why the referential reading should be more readily available to odin than to dva indefinites. The answer cannot lie in the fact that phonologically reduced odin is often used as a marker of specificity (see section 2.4), given that we see exactly the same difference between odin and dva even when the numeral is contrastively focused. The answer also cannot lie in unavailability of referential readings for higher numerals: if referential readings (whether derived via choice functions or by some other means) are in principle available to indefinites, they should be available to all numeral indefinites to the same extent. We hypothesize that the answer lies in pragmatics, specifically, in the reasons for choosing to use one vs. two.
simplified_text:
Why is it easier for "odin" to be used as a referential reading than for "dva"? 

It's not because "odin" is often used to show that something is specific. We see the same difference between "odin" and "dva" even when the number is emphasized. 

It's also not because higher numbers can't be used as referential readings. If we can use a number to show that something is specific, we should be able to use any number to do the same. 

We think the answer is that it has to do with how we choose to use one or two.

complex_text: For some of the syntactic feature conflicts we examine (as we shall see in later sections), there are speakers with principled resolution in favor of the conjunct nearest the factor; for these speakers, resolution by principle will mask the effects of other sorts of resolution. Other speakers have different principles for resolution in certain situations-e.g. resolving the conflict in 5 in favor of 3rd person and plural number. For them, 5 is acceptable with are, but not with am or is: Either they or I are going to have to go. Again, principled resolution will mask the effects of other sorts of resolution.
simplified_text:
Some people have a simple way to solve problems with words. They choose the word that is closest to the main word. This way, they can ignore other problems with words. 

Other people have different ways to solve problems with words. For example, they might choose the word that is in the 3rd person (like "he" or "she") and plural (like "they" instead of "I"). For these people, the sentence "Either they or I are going to have to go" is okay, but not the sentence "Either am or is going to have to go". They have a simple way to solve problems, and it hides other problems.

complex_text: Summing up, the notion of ‘topic’ (without ‘contrastive’) should be used with great caution. There is no agreed-upon way to identify topics across languages, and therefore inconsistent claims about their properties abound. There is a semi-formal rendering of the notion of aboutness topic, which, however, relies on a notion of context which cannot be reliably established independently. This section can be read as a plea to refrain from using the notion altogether, and characterize ‘topic’-markings independently along the lines suggested in the questions above.

This section is saying that maybe we shouldn't use the term "topic" at all, and instead describe what makes a topic special in our own way.

complex_text: The first goal of this article is to establish whether Russian scope is frozen ; only for SVO order, as Bobaljik and Wurmbrand’s account predicts; or only for OVS order, as suggested by Bailyn ) or whether surface scope is only a preference. We hypothesize that Russian does not have frozen scope and that the appearance of frozen scope is due to a strong processing-based preference. This is Hypothesis 1 in , no disagreement on this point has ever been reported, and an experimental study by Scontras et al. found zero acceptance of sentences equivalent to One shark attacked every pirate in contexts matching the inverse scope reading.
simplified_text: Is Russian grammar special? We want to know if Russian grammar is different only when the sentence starts with the doer (SVO) or only when it starts with the thing being acted upon (OVS). Or is Russian grammar different because of how our brains process language? We think Russian grammar is not special and that it seems special because of how our brains work. This is our first idea. Nobody has ever disagreed with this idea, and a study by Scontras and others found that people don't accept sentences that seem to have this special grammar. For example, "One shark attacked every pirate" doesn't sound right.

Topic, Focus, and Local Relevance

This chapter is about how we talk and understand what we're talking about. It's like focusing on a specific topic and making sure everyone knows what we're talking about.

The author, von Stechow, explains that when we talk, we often use certain words or phrases to help us understand what we're talking about. These words or phrases are like a "topic" that helps us stay on track.

The author also talks about "focus" which is like shining a light on a specific part of the topic. This helps us understand what's important and what's not.

Finally, the author discusses "local relevance" which is like making sure what we're saying is connected to what we're talking about. This helps us understand how what we're saying fits into the bigger picture.

Overall, this chapter is about how we use language to communicate and make sure we're all on the same page.

complex_text: Finally, we ask whether reconstruction is necessary for deriving inverse scope of OVS sentences under contrastive focus: could the effect instead be due to covert QR? We argue that the answer is no, for three reasons. First, if covert QR were at work, we would have to make a very unintuitive claim, namely, that focusing the scrambled object leads to covert QR of the postverbal subject (as opposed to reconstruction of the object itself). Second, covert QR would not be able to account for the difference between SVO and OVS sentences: it should be possible for the postverbal QP to undergo QR to a position above the preverbal QP, regardless of the structural configuration. And third, if contrastive focus facilitated covert QR, it would be most natural to suppose that it should facilitate covert QR of the focused QP, that is, the postverbal indefinite in (15c–d). However, we have seen that contrastive prosody had no effect on the sentence types in (15c–d).

Is it possible that the way we phrase sentences can be changed without really changing the sentence itself? We think not, for three reasons. First, if we were to make a change without changing the sentence, we would have to say something weird, like "making the object the main focus of the sentence makes the subject disappear". Second, this change wouldn't work the same way for all types of sentences. And third, if focusing a word made it disappear, it would be more likely to happen to the word we're focusing, not to the other word. But we've already seen that changing the way we say a sentence doesn't change the sentence itself.

complex_text: 8 To distinguish forms of the verb paradigm, we use the feature VFORM of Gazdar et al. 1985. 9 We thus follow Huddleston 1975 in steering a middle course on the question of the relationship between words and word forms. Although we do not necessarily echo Huddleston's decisions with regard to particular forms, we agree with him that we cannot take formal identity to entail word identity; this would mean that there were no cases of ambiguity and no cases of homophony. We also agree with him that formal distinctness in one paradigm cannot be taken to entail word dis- tinctness in all related paradigms, which would mean treating the modal can as three distinct but homophonous words because of the formal distinctness of am/are/is-or even worse, assuming that there are distinct words for each combination of values for features relevant in the language, which would mean treating the modal can as six distinct but homophonous words.

We also agree with Huddleston that being different in one group of words doesn't always mean being different in all related groups. For example, we can't say that the word "can" has six different meanings just because it has different forms.

complex_text: We further observe that, to the best of our knowledge, the difference between SVO and OVS orders when it comes to the effects of contrastive focus is something never before noted in the literature on Russian scope. We have reason to believe that this difference would not be revealed via introspection and informal judgments. Our data show that there is quite a bit of variability in speakers’ judgments, and if a researcher relied only on introspection and on gathering informal judgments, it is not clear whether any definitive conclusion could be reached. However, with controlled testing and statistical analysis, the SVO/OVS asymmetry with regard to contrastive focus has proven to be quite robust and has held up across different versions of our test. To conclude, we see this article as a first step in an experimental investigation of how information structure, prosody, and processing preferences influence quantifier scope crosslinguistically.

simplified_text:
Here is the rewritten text:

The results are shown in Table 3. We found that the main effects came from two things: the type of sentence and the type of numbers used. 

* The type of sentence: People rated sentences that started with a universal word (like "all") higher than sentences that started with an indefinite word (like "some"). This makes sense because universal words are always true, while indefinite words can be true or false.
* The type of numbers: People rated sentences with numbers that had a specific value (like "2") higher than sentences with numbers that had a general value (like "some"). This is because people are more likely to understand sentences with specific numbers.

We also found that there were interactions between the type of sentence, the order of the words, and the way the sentence was spoken. This means that the way people rated the sentences depended on these factors. 

* The type of numbers didn't affect how people rated the sentences, even though people generally liked sentences with specific numbers better. This means that the same patterns were seen in sentences with specific numbers as in sentences with general numbers.
* There was no main effect of the list, which means that the order of the sentences didn't affect how people rated them.

complex_text: Our study was not designed to tease apart the referential and quantificational readings of odin indefinites, and more empirical work is required on this point (see section 4). However, as discussed above, the comparison between odin and dva indefinites provides evidence for the existence of both readings. On the one hand, the existence of the quantificational readings of indefinites, coupled with a processing-based preference for surface scope, accounts for the similarities in the behavior of odin and dva indefinites. On the other hand, the availability of a referential reading for odin accounts for why odin indefinites are overall accepted less in the distributive context than are dva indefinites.

On the other hand, the fact that "some" words can also have a different meaning explains why "some" words are less likely to be used in a certain situation compared to "both" words.

Bu¨ring’s analysis is specifically about the contrastive topic-focus configuration; when only the preverbal QP is in focus (the German equivalent of ALL politicians are not corrupt), according to Bu¨ring the surface scope reading is freely available, even preferred. complex_text: A common way of modelling aboutness topics is to break the meaning of sentences with topics into two parts: the referent of the topic marked expression calls the Pragmatic Assertion of a sentence. The discourse context, too, is structured into individuals, and properties that are agreed to hold of them. The standard metaphor for this is a stack of indexed ﬁle cards . For concreteness, associate a context C with a partial function fC from individuals to sets of properties, i. e. a set of of pairs ⟨x, Φ⟩, where x is an individual (or a discourse referent) and Φ is a set of properties. So for any individual i that is part of the context, fC(i) gives us the set of all properties
simplified_text:
Here's the rewritten text:

When we try to understand what a sentence is talking about, we can break it down into two parts. The first part is what the sentence is saying about a specific thing, like a person or an object. We call this part the "pragmatic assertion" of the sentence.

The context of the sentence is also made up of individual things and the properties that are true about them. A good way to think about this is to imagine a stack of index cards. Each card has a name or a description of something, and it also has a list of characteristics or traits that go with it.

Let's use an example to make this clearer. Imagine we're talking about a specific person, like a friend. We can think of the context as a special list that says what's true about that person. This list would have the person's name, and it would also have a list of things that are true about them, like their favorite food or their favorite hobby. So, if we look at the list for that person, it would show us all the things that are true about them.

complex_text: Russian scrambling, or constituent reordering, while receiving less attention than scrambling in, say, German or Japanese, has been investigated by a number of researchers . Here, we discuss possible derivations of both SVO and OVS sentences containing quantifiers in subject and object position, and the corresponding consequences for scope interpretation. We assume that in principle, the scope configuration can be affected by reconstruction as well as by covert QR. In this section, we lay out the logical possibilities of how inverse scope can be derived via reconstruction or via covert QR. In section 2.3, we will address how these possibilities relate to the role of IS in Russian.
simplified_text: Russian sentence structure, or how words are arranged in a sentence, is important to study. Many researchers have looked into how Russian sentences are structured. Here, we'll talk about how sentences with special words (quantifiers) in the subject and object positions can be arranged in different ways, and how this affects how we understand the sentence. We think that the way the sentence is structured can be changed in two ways: one is by using a hidden rule, and the other is by using a special rule that is not spoken. In this section, we'll explain the different ways this can happen. Later, we'll discuss how these ways relate to the role of a special word (IS) in Russian.

There are thus unresolved (and potentially pertinent) questions among the CT-alternatives, as required by CIR.

We are at present examining scope with SOV and OSV word orders, to see if the same patterns hold as with SVO and OSV word orders . Another direction for future study would be to experimentally test scope readings in double object constructions, where, according to Antonyuk-Yudina , scope is frozen .

complex_text: On the methodological level, our study highlights the value of doing controlled experiments when it comes to studying interface phenomena. While simple acceptability judgments of written sentences have been found to yield highly similar results whether administered formally or informally , this is arguably not the case for phenomena that fall at the interface between syntax, discourse, and prosody. Indeed, in the case of Russian scope, prior accounts relying on introspection and informal judgments have disagreed on the judgments: Ionin has argued that Russian scope is frozen, Antonyuk has argued that it is not, and Bailyn has tentatively suggested that it is frozen for OVS but not for SVO order.
simplified_text:
Here is the rewritten text:

When we study how language works, it's important to do controlled experiments. This means we need to be careful and make sure we're testing the same things in the same way. This is important because some language things are hard to study. For example, when we look at how Russian sentences work, some people have different ideas about what's right and what's wrong. Some people think Russian sentences are frozen, which means they don't change much. Others think they do change. It's hard to know for sure!

complex_text: The ‘head shake’ is perhaps the most well known of all head gestures, apart from the ‘nod’, and it is generally recognized, at least in cultures of Western European derivation, as a way of saying ‘no.’ It is often used as a gestural equivalent of this particle and, in many contexts, it can be directly translated by this. In virtue of this, indeed, the head shake could be regarded as a true ‘emblem’ in the well-known deﬁnition of Ekman and Friesen . According to this deﬁnition, to qualify as an ‘emblem’ a gesture must have “a direct verbal translation or dictionary deﬁnition, usually consisting of a word or two or perhaps a phrase.” Yet, when we collect observations on when people employ the head shake, we ﬁnd that its use is far from being conﬁned to cases where it is the equivalent of some easily rendered verbal expression such as ‘No.’ Often, the only way in which its meaning may be rendered is by re-formulating
simplified_text:
The "head shake" is a very common head gesture, and most people recognize it as a way to say "no". It's like a special way to say "no" without using words. In many cases, it can be translated to "no". Because of this, the head shake could be considered a true "emblem", which is a special kind of gesture that has a clear meaning. 

However, when we look at when people use the head shake, we find that it's not just used to say "no". Sometimes, it's used in other ways, and its meaning can't be easily explained with just a few words.

complex_text: Let us focus on the available construal ﬁrst: The sentence asserts that not all politicians are corrupt, and its CT-alternatives will be ‘Is it false that Q politicians are corrupt?’, for example ‘Is it false that many politicians are corrupt?’. This question is not resolved by the assertion, and it is plausibly pertinent, meeting the CIR. On the other construal, the sentence says that all politicians are uncorrupt, with CT-alternatives like ‘Are Q politicians corrupt?’. But since the sentence asserts that none of them are, any such question is resolved by the assertion alone. As was the case with (19), this construal of (21) cannot possibly meet the CT Interpretation Rule (7). But in (21), unlike in (19), there is a second construal which is (or can be) felicitous, so that the sentence is acceptable, though not ambiguous, with CT+F marking.

The sentence says that not all politicians are corrupt. This makes us think of other questions, like "Are many politicians corrupt?" This question is not answered by the sentence, and it's a good question to ask. 

On the other hand, the sentence could be saying that all politicians are not corrupt. This makes us think of questions like "Are any politicians corrupt?" But since the sentence says none of them are corrupt, these questions are already answered. 

This is different from another sentence (19) because this sentence has a second way of looking at it that makes sense. So, the sentence is okay, even though it's not ambiguous, and it can be marked with CT+F.

complex_text: 3.5.2 Contrastive Focus and Reconstruction Contrastive focus was found to facilitate inverse scope in Russian, supporting Hypothesis 2 in (14). However, it had a significant effect only on OVS order, though numerically, there was a small effect with SVO order as well. Coming back to the different predictions in (17), we see that our findings concerning the effect of reconstruction are most consistent with (17d): a focused preverbal object reconstructs to a position in the scope of the subject, leading to inverse scope (variant 2 in table 2), while a focused preverbal subject reconstructs only as far as Spec,TP, hence still taking scope over the object (variant 2 in table 1).
simplified_text:
Contrastive Focus and Reconstruction

Contrastive focus helps with word order in Russian. It makes a big difference in one type of sentence, but a small difference in another. 

Our results match what was predicted in (17). When we focus on a word that comes before the verb, it moves to a position where it can be affected by the subject. This makes the subject's scope bigger. But when we focus on a word that comes before the subject, it only moves a little and still has scope over the object.

complex_text: A thorough look at descriptions of topic markings in various languages reveals that diﬀerences like these abound, making it impossible to talk about properties of ‘topics’ in general. An investigation of a suspected topic-marking in a given language should therefore start, not by assuming that, since it is a topic, it has a particular cluster of properties, but by establishing their exact speciﬁc properties independently, among them. . .
simplified_text:
When we look at how different languages use topic markings, we see that each language is unique. This makes it hard to talk about what all topic markings have in common. 

When we study a topic marking in a specific language, we shouldn't start by assuming it has certain characteristics just because it's a topic marking. Instead, we should start by learning exactly what it does and how it works.

complex_text: 3 We discuss only movement accounts of scrambling here, and do not consider accounts of scrambling as basegeneration, such as Bo'kovic´ and Takahashi’s . On Bo'kovic´ and Takahashi’s account, the scrambled DP undergoes postsyntactic lowering at LF to its thematic position. There are many arguments against the base-generation approach . The most relevant argument for our purposes has to do with scope: as Bailyn discusses, the base-generation account predicts that scrambled sentences will not have object  subject scope readings, since the scrambled object DP is interpreted in its thematic position, that is, in the scope of the subject. Given that this is manifestly a wrong prediction for Russian—all accounts of Russian scope agree that surface, object  subject scope is the most readily available reading for scrambled OVS sentences—we do not consider the base-generation account further.
simplified_text:
We're only going to talk about one way that words can move around in a sentence, called "scrambling". We're not going to discuss another way that some people think happens, which is called "base-generation".

Some people, like Bo'kovic and Takahashi, think that when a sentence is scrambled, the words move to a new position at the very end of the sentence. But there are many reasons to disagree with this idea.

One big problem is that it doesn't make sense for some sentences. For example, in Russian, it's very common for a sentence to have a certain reading where the object is in the scope of the subject. But if the base-generation idea is right, it would mean that this reading is not possible, which is not true. Since this is a clear problem, we're not going to talk about the base-generation idea anymore.
